hanging on with bulldog determination.


On Thursday, October %, the jour
neymen plumbers to the number of 20
struck for an increase in wages from
IS to $3.50 day. The loss to them
at the old rate of wages will soon
mount up to the round sum of $4,000.
During the time since they first struck
and the present the men have drawn
heavily on their own personal resources,
as well as upon their treasury.


With the bosses still firm in their
determination of holding out until
spring if necessary. it is evident set
Element will soon be forced upon the
plumbers themselves or the union will
he disrupted.


This strike has caused more ill-feel-
ing among the members of organized
labor than is generally known. The
trades council itself is badly split up
owing to the withdrawal Of the ear
penters, and there are several other
unions which only granted their en-
do.sement of the strike under pres
sure brought to bear by other unions.
